title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about a teacher who uses an unconventional approach to education in a rural school. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Outside the Classroom
content.subtitle: A magazine article about a primary school teacher whose methods challenge conventional ideas about learning.
content.text: The first thing you notice about James Whitfield's classroom is that it is rarely used. On most mornings, if you visit his school in the Welsh countryside, you will find his class of twenty-eight children scattered across the school's surrounding fields and woodland, engaged in activities that at first glance appear to bear little resemblance to a national curriculum lesson. [31] On closer inspection — and this is the point Whitfield would insist upon — every activity is precisely mapped to a learning objective, executed in an environment that measurably improves children's ability to absorb and retain information. [/31] || Whitfield has been teaching for nineteen years, the last eight at this school. He came to outdoor learning gradually, after growing frustrated with what he describes as 'the narrowing'. 'I watched children come in excited and curious,' he told me, 'and I watched the system work to contain that. Not maliciously — the system isn't malicious. But curiosity doesn't sit still, and classrooms require stillness.' [32] His initial experiments with outdoor sessions were modest: a mathematics lesson using sticks and stones, a literacy activity structured around observational writing in the school garden. [/32] The results, as measured by the children's engagement and the quality of their work, were immediately encouraging. || The research base that now supports Whitfield's instincts is substantial. Studies conducted across Scandinavia, Germany and Japan — where outdoor learning programmes have a much longer institutional history than in the UK — consistently show that children who receive a significant proportion of their education outdoors demonstrate higher levels of physical health, improved concentration, greater emotional resilience, and in several studies, better academic outcomes in standard testing. [33] The mechanism is not fully understood, but there is growing evidence that physical movement, natural light, and contact with living systems activate neural processes that sedentary indoor learning suppresses. [/33] || Not everyone is persuaded. Some colleagues, Whitfield acknowledges, worry that time spent outside is time away from the structured content delivery they consider essential preparation for standardised tests. [34] This tension — between a model of education organised around measurable outputs and one organised around broader developmental outcomes — runs through every debate about school reform, and outdoor learning has become a focal point for it. [/34] Whitfield does not dismiss these concerns, but he disputes their premise. The choice, he argues, is not between outdoor learning and academic achievement — his children's test results are consistently strong. The choice is about what you believe education is for. || The children themselves, when asked, are refreshingly direct about their preferences. [35] Almost without exception, they identify the outdoor sessions as the times when they feel they are doing something real — a distinction they seem instinctively to understand even if they cannot fully articulate it. [/35] || Whitfield is beginning to write up his methods for a wider professional audience. He is careful not to overstate his conclusions. [36] 'I'm not saying this works for every child in every context,' he said. 'I'm saying that when we assume four walls and a desk are the default conditions of learning, we should at least ask why.' [/36]

questions.question: The writer says 'this is the point Whitfield would insist upon' in order to
options: A) contrast the children's apparent freedom with the rigour of Whitfield's planning.
B) warn readers not to judge Whitfield's lessons by their appearance alone.
C) argue that outdoor learning is more structured than indoor classroom lessons.
D) confirm that Whitfield's approach has been approved by education authorities.
questions.explanation: The writer uses this phrase to highlight that while activities look unstructured from the outside, Whitfield's key point is that each one is carefully planned — contrasting appearance with reality.

questions.question: What does Whitfield mean when he describes a process he calls 'the narrowing'?
options: A) The reduction in the number of subjects taught in primary schools.
B) The way the school system gradually suppresses children's natural curiosity.
C) The increasing emphasis on testing rather than creative activities.
D) The difficulty of managing classes of children with different learning needs.
questions.explanation: Whitfield describes watching children arrive 'excited and curious' and then the system working 'to contain that', explaining that 'curiosity doesn't sit still, and classrooms require stillness' — the system narrows children's natural learning impulse.

questions.question: The international research mentioned in the third paragraph is used to show that
options: A) outdoor learning is most effective when it is introduced in early childhood.
B) Whitfield's approach is supported by evidence from multiple countries and settings.
C) Scandinavian educational systems are generally superior to the British one.
D) physical exercise is the most important factor in improving academic results.
questions.explanation: The research from Scandinavia, Germany and Japan 'consistently' supports outdoor learning benefits — validating Whitfield's instincts with an international evidence base across multiple contexts.

questions.question: The 'tension' the writer refers to in the fourth paragraph is between
options: A) Whitfield's teaching methods and his school's management team.
B) the needs of children who enjoy outdoor learning and those who prefer classrooms.
C) educational approaches focused on measurable results and those with wider aims.
D) the requirements of national tests and the content of the school curriculum.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly describes the tension 'between a model of education organised around measurable outputs and one organised around broader developmental outcomes' — a philosophical disagreement about education's purpose.

questions.question: When the children describe outdoor sessions as times when they feel they are doing 'something real', they suggest that
options: A) they prefer physical activities to academic work.
B) indoor classroom activities feel less meaningful to them.
C) they understand what they are learning better when outside.
D) outdoor work is more challenging than classroom-based learning.
questions.explanation: The children's instinctive distinction between 'something real' and other activities implies that indoor classroom work feels less genuine or relevant to them by contrast.

questions.question: Whitfield's final question — about why four walls and a desk are assumed to be defaults — is intended to
options: A) suggest that traditional classrooms should be abolished.
B) challenge the unexamined assumptions behind conventional schooling.
C) demonstrate that his own approach is superior to all other teaching methods.
D) encourage other teachers to adopt his methods in their own schools.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical question targets the unquestioned assumption that classrooms are the natural environment for learning, asking others to examine why they accept this without question.
